content.content: 1 # jjezs_paddock ₣A9 2 3 ## Character 4 5 Product design into prototype implementation. Starts with synthesis (specs capturing a rich design conversation), transitions to exploratory engineering (Tauri + Rust + cross-platform clipboard), then steady implementation of a three-tier PHI detection engine. Requires careful attention to minting discipline — this kit lives alongside RBK/JJK/BUK and must respect the prefix universe. 6 7 ## Problem 8 9 Clinicians using Epic EHR need to consult Open Evidence (an AI diagnostic tool) but must not paste Protected Health Information (PHI) into it. HIPAA Safe Harbor defines 18 PHI categories that must be removed. Today, clinicians either skip the consultation or manually scrub text — both are unacceptable. No clipboard-level tool exists to automate the triage. 10 11 ## Solution 12 13 Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that: 14 1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption 15 2. Parses Epic "Copy All" HTML for structural context (labels, sections) 16 3. Runs a three-tier PHI detection engine (regex → label-anchored → dictionary) 17 4. Displays a triage view: RED (definite PHI), YELLOW (questionable), GREY (pass) 18 5. Lets clinician toggle findings — YELLOW easily, RED with deliberate friction (separate panel section) 19 6. Writes anonymized plain text to clipboard with category-specific placeholders (DOB→age, >89→"90+") 20 7. Self-updates from `/Users/Shared/apcua/` without confirmation 21 22 ## Key Design Decisions 23 24 - **Anchored vs Matched**: Tier 2 (label position) and Tier 3 (dictionary identity) are independent mechanisms with distinct vocabulary — see APCPS 25 - **Case-insensitive**: All dictionary lookups normalize to lowercase; original case preserved in display 26 - **Per-word anchoring**: No multi-word merge — each word individually classified 27 - **Conservative bias**: False positive preferred over false negative 28 - **Provider/facility names**: Treated as PHI (re-identifying in combination with clinical details) 29 - **Clipboard always cleared** after consumption 30 - **Clinical content heuristic** gates processing; non-clinical clipboard shows diagnostic and clears triage 31 32 ## Authoritative Documents 33 34 | Document | Owns | Path | 35 |----------|------|------| 36 | Product Spec (APCS) | UX plan, workflow, deployment, HIPAA context | `Tools/apck/APCS-Specification.md` | 37 | Prototype Spec (APCPS) | Detection pipeline, tech stack, dictionaries, project structure, scope boundaries | `Tools/apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` | 38 | Claude Context | Build/run/deploy instructions, file mappings, prefix tree, tabtargets | `Tools/apck/apck-claude-context.md` | 39 | Rust Coding Guide (RCG) | Naming conventions, test patterns, disciplines | `Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md` | 40 41 # jjezs_pace ₢A9AAD 42 43 ## Character 44 45 Algorithmic — transform semi-structured HTML into a representation the detection engine can consume. 46 47 ## Deliverables 48 49 - `apcd/src/apcrp_parse.rs` — Parse Epic HTML using `scraper`. Extract: 50 - Labeled fields: identify `Label: value` patterns, emit (label, value) pairs 51 - Section boundaries: detect Epic section headers (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, A/P, Signature) 52 - Freetext runs: narrative text between labels/sections 53 - Preserve source position information so findings can map back to the original text for rendering 54 - Define data structures for parsed output: a sequence of typed spans (LabeledField, SectionHeader, Narrative, etc.) each carrying their text content and source offset 55 - Plain text extraction path: strip all HTML, preserve line breaks and section structure 56 - `apcd/src/apctp_parse.rs` — unit tests: feed synthetic fixture HTML through parser, assert correct extraction of labeled fields, section boundaries, and narrative spans 57 58 ## Verification 59 60 - Unit tests pass via `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` 61 - Feed the synthetic fixture through the parser. Print structured output showing detected labels, sections, and narrative spans with positions. All labeled PHI fields (Patient, DOB, MRN, Attending, Facility, etc.) correctly identified as LabeledField spans. 62

content.content: 1 # Ann's PHI Clipbuddy — Prototype Specification 2 3 ## Scope 4 5 This document specifies the engineering pipeline for the proof-of-life prototype. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience — see [APCS-Specification.md](APCS-Specification.md) for the UX plan. 6 7 This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec. 8 9 ## Scope Boundaries 10 11 The prototype deliberately excludes: 12 13 - **No persistence** — no logs, no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes 14 - **One patient at a time** — no batch processing, no queue 15 - **No configuration** — no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI 16 - **No click-to-highlight** — clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature) 17 - **No multi-word merge** — adjacent name hits are individually flagged, not merged into a single token 18 19 ## Tech Stack 20 21 | Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale | 22 |-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------| 23 | Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) | 24 | HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever | 25 | Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size | 26 | Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns | 27 | Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection | 28 | GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | HTML/CSS/JS frontend, Rust backend, system webview | 29 | File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows | 30 31 All engine crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows — both ship with the OS). 32 33 ## Input: Epic "Copy All" HTML 34 35 Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: 36 37 - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata 38 - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) 39 - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections 40 - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps 41 42 The HTML structure is semi-structured — not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. This structure is the primary advantage over plain text processing: labels anchor PHI detection with high precision. 43 44 ## Clipboard Handling 45 46 ### Clinical Content Heuristic 47 48 Before processing, the engine checks whether clipboard content appears to be clinical. The check scans for known Epic/clinical label patterns: 49 50 **Positive indicators** (presence of 2+ triggers acceptance): 51 - `Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`, `Facility:` 52 - `Chief Complaint:`, `History of Present Illness:`, `Assessment/Plan:` 53 - `Vitals:`, `Labs:`, `Medications:`, `Allergies:` 54 - HTML `Label:` structure characteristic of Epic formatting 55 56 If clinical content is detected, the engine proceeds to the detection pipeline and clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via `arboard::set_text("")`). 57 58 If clinical content is NOT detected, the app clears any previous triage state and returns to the initial instruction view. The app displays a brief diagnostic line showing: clipboard content length, content type (HTML/plain text), and the first ~100 characters of content. This diagnostic aids debugging when real Epic data fails the heuristic — the developer can see what was on the clipboard and update the label list accordingly. 59 60 This heuristic will be refined when tested against real Epic output. The initial label list is calibrated to pass the synthetic test fixture. 61 62 ### Clipboard Change Detection 63 64 On window focus, the engine compares the current clipboard content (byte-for-byte string comparison) against the last successfully consumed content. If identical, the existing triage state is preserved — no reprocessing. If different, the clinical content heuristic runs on the new content. Clinical notes are a few KB; storing the last consumed string for comparison has negligible memory cost at prototype scale. 65 66 ## Detection Pipeline 67 68 ### Overview 69 70 Three tiers execute in sequence. Results merge by highest severity — if a token is flagged by multiple tiers, the most severe classification wins. 71 72 ``` 73 Input: Epic HTML clipboard content 74 | 75 Parse HTML → typed spans with labels and positions 76 | 77 Tier 1: Regex scan → RED 78 | 79 Tier 2: Label-anchored extraction → RED 80 | 81 Tier 3: Dictionary blacklist/whitelist scan → YELLOW or PASS 82 | 83 Merge by highest severity 84 | 85 Output: classified token stream → frontend 86 ``` 87 88 ### Tier 1 — Regex Patterns (→ RED) 89 90 Structural patterns with low false positive rate. No database dependency. 91 92 **Known ambiguities:** The zip code regex (`\d{5}`) can match 5-digit lab values, identifiers, or dosage amounts. The date regex can match version numbers or other formatted numerics. The street address regex can match non-address text with number-word-suffix patterns. These are accepted risks for the prototype — the conservative bias (false positive > false negative) means these appear as RED items the clinician can review. Context-aware refinement is deferred. 93 94 | Pattern | Category | Regex (representative) | Notes | 95 |---------|----------|----------------------|-------| 96 | SSN | `[SSN]` | `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}` | | 97 | Phone | `[PHONE]` | `\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{4}`, `\(\d{3}\)\s?\d{3}-\d{4}` | Multiple formats | 98 | Email | `[EMAIL]` | `[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+` | | 99 | Dates | `[DATE]` | `\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{2,4}`, `\d{1,2}-\d{1,2}-\d{2,4}`, month name patterns | Multiple formats | 100 | Street address | `[ADDRESS]` | `\d+\s+[A-Z]\w+\s+(St\|Dr\|Ave\|Blvd\|Rd\|Ln\|Way\|Ct\|Pl\|Cir\|Drive\|Street\|Avenue\|Boulevard\|Road\|Lane\|Court\|Place\|Circle)` | Followed by optional city/state/zip | 101 | Zip code | `[ADDRESS]` | `\b\d{5}(-\d{4})?\b` | Within address context | 102 | MRN (labeled) | `[MRN]` | Value following `MRN:` label | | 103 | Account (labeled) | `[ACCOUNT]` | Value following `Account` or `Acct` label | | 104 | Encounter ID (labeled) | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | Value following `Encounter ID:` label | | 105 106 Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`. 107 108 ### Tier 2 — Label-Anchored Extraction (→ RED) 109 110 Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. Spawn `open -n ` to launch new version 219 3. `std::process::exit(0)` to terminate current process 220 221 No confirmation dialog. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience — see [APCS-Specification.md](APCS-Specification.md) for the UX plan. This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec. ## Scope Boundaries The prototype deliberately excludes: - **No persistence** — no logs, no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes - **One patient at a time** — no batch processing, no queue - **No configuration** — no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI - **No click-to-highlight** — clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature) - **No multi-word merge** — adjacent name hits are individually flagged, not merged into a single token ## Tech Stack | Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale | |-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------| | Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) | | HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever | | Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size | | Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns | | Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection | | GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | HTML/CSS/JS frontend, Rust backend, system webview | | File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows | All engine crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows — both ship with the OS). ## Input: Epic "Copy All" HTML Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps The HTML structure is semi-structured — not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. This structure is the primary advantage over plain text processing: labels anchor PHI detection with high precision. ## Clipboard Handling ### Clinical Content Heuristic Before processing, the engine checks whether clipboard content appears to be clinical. The check scans for known Epic/clinical label patterns: **Positive indicators** (presence of 2+ triggers acceptance): - `Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`, `Facility:` - `Chief Complaint:`, `History of Present Illness:`, `Assessment/Plan:` - `Vitals:`, `Labs:`, `Medications:`, `Allergies:` - HTML `Label:` structure characteristic of Epic formatting If clinical content is detected, the engine proceeds to the detection pipeline and clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via `arboard::set_text("")`). If clinical content is NOT detected, the app clears any previous triage state and returns to the initial instruction view. The app displays a brief diagnostic line showing: clipboard content length, content type (HTML/plain text), and the first ~100 characters of content. This diagnostic aids debugging when real Epic data fails the heuristic — the developer can see what was on the clipboard and update the label list accordingly. This heuristic will be refined when tested against real Epic output. The initial label list is calibrated to pass the synthetic test fixture. ### Clipboard Change Detection On window focus, the engine compares the current clipboard content (byte-for-byte string comparison) against the last successfully consumed content. If identical, the existing triage state is preserved — no reprocessing. If different, the clinical content heuristic runs on the new content. Clinical notes are a few KB; storing the last consumed string for comparison has negligible memory cost at prototype scale. ## Detection Pipeline ### Overview Three tiers execute in sequence. Results merge by highest severity — if a token is flagged by multiple tiers, the most severe classification wins. ``` Input: Epic HTML clipboard content | Parse HTML → typed spans with labels and positions | Tier 1: Regex scan → RED | Tier 2: Label-anchored extraction → RED | Tier 3: Dictionary blacklist/whitelist scan → YELLOW or PASS | Merge by highest severity | Output: classified token stream → frontend ``` ### Tier 1 — Regex Patterns (→ RED) Structural patterns with low false positive rate.
